title: Classifying Indications and Documenting Results
narrationScript:
Say a weld sits in front of you and something catches your eye. What you do in the next five minutes, and what you write in the next thirty, is the entire value of visual testing. Finding things is half the job. Calling them correctly and recording them so they stand up later is the other half.

Get the vocabulary straight first, because the words carry legal weight. Anything that attracts your attention is an indication. Nothing more. An indication becomes meaningful only after interpretation, when you figure out what caused it. Some indications are false, caused by the surface or the setup, like a smear of grease that reads as a crack until you wipe it. Some are nonrelevant, real features that do not threaten the part, like a machining mark the drawing allows. The rest are relevant. They come from actual discontinuities. Relevant indications get evaluated, which means measured and compared against the acceptance criteria your procedure invokes. Only a relevant indication that fails that comparison earns the word defect. A defect is rejectable by definition. Use the word that way. Calling every blemish a defect on paper can condemn good hardware and start fights nobody needs.

Acceptance criteria are the ruler. They come from the code, the contract, or the drawing, and the procedure tells you which set applies. Criteria read like this. No cracks, period. Undercut allowed up to a stated depth. Porosity allowed up to a stated size and count over a stated length. Reinforcement allowed up to a stated height. Your job is to measure honestly, with the right tool. A fillet gauge for leg and throat size. An undercut gauge or a bridge cam gauge for depth and reinforcement. A pit gauge for corrosion. A scale or a tape for lengths and spacing. If the call is close, measure again. And remember that as a Level One you examine and record. The final accept or reject usually belongs to a Level Two or above, and your written practice spells that out.

Now the part that outlives you. The report. Years from now, someone may pull your report to decide whether a crack grew or whether it was always there. The report works only if it stands on its own. So it carries the date and your name, signature, and certification level. It identifies the exact part, by serial number, heat number, weld number, or drawing reference. It names the procedure and revision you worked to, and the acceptance criteria you measured against. It states the technique, direct or remote, and it proves the conditions, meaning the light meter reading at the surface, the meter serial number, and its calibration due date. Then it states the results. Every recordable indication gets a type, a size, and a location tied to a datum someone else can find. Four hundred fifteen millimeters from the north end of weld twelve. Not near the top. Accepted or rejected, say which, and say against what.

Words alone locate things poorly, so add a sketch or a photograph. Put a scale in the photo, a ruler or a gauge, and note where the camera stood. On the sketch, draw the datum and dimension the indication from it. Mark the hardware itself when the procedure allows, with an approved marker, never a sharp scribe on anything that lives under stress.

Write it once and write it clean. If you make an error on a paper form, draw a single line through it, write the correction beside it, and initial and date it. No scribbles. No white-out. A covered number looks exactly like a covered mistake. Keep every page legible and complete, and turn records in to be retained for as long as the contract and the quality system require, which can be decades.

Why all the ceremony? Because the report is the inspection. An exam without a record never happened, as far as the code is concerned. Records are the baseline the next in-service inspection compares against. They are the evidence in a warranty dispute, and the trail an investigator follows after a failure. Inspect like a hawk, then write like an accountant. Do both, and your work will hold up under any light anyone ever shines on it.

sections.heading: Indication, Interpretation, Evaluation — In That Order
sections.body:
Visual testing has a chain of custody for observations, and the words are precise because they carry contractual weight.

An **indication** is anything that draws your attention — a line, a spot, a color change. Finding one starts a process; it does not end one.

**Interpretation** asks: what caused this? Three answers are possible:

- **False indication** — caused by surface condition or setup, not the part: a grease smear, a water stain, a shadow. Correct the cause, clean, and re-examine.
- **Nonrelevant indication** — caused by a real feature that is there by design or is otherwise harmless: a machining mark the drawing allows, a permitted weld ripple. Record it if the procedure says to.
- **Relevant indication** — caused by an actual discontinuity. These move on to evaluation.

**Evaluation** measures the relevant indication and compares it against the **acceptance criteria** the procedure invokes. Two outcomes: it meets the criteria and is accepted — and recorded — or it exceeds them.

Only then does the last word apply: a **defect** is a discontinuity whose type, size, or location makes it rejectable under the governing criteria. Every defect is a discontinuity; very few discontinuities are defects. Skipping the chain — calling a smudge a crack, or an in-limit undercut a defect — condemns good hardware, buys unnecessary repairs, and erodes the credibility of every call you make afterward.

sections.caption: Interpret first, evaluate second: only a relevant indication that exceeds the acceptance criteria earns the word defect.

sections.heading: Measuring Against the Criteria
sections.body:
Acceptance criteria come from the code, the contract, or the drawing — the procedure tells you which set governs. A typical workmanship set reads like this: **no cracks or incomplete fusion** of any size; **undercut** not exceeding a stated depth, often 1/32 in. (0.8 mm); **porosity** limited by individual pore size and by count or spacing over a stated length; **reinforcement height** capped by thickness range; **arc strikes** and unremoved **spatter** prohibited or limited.

Close calls are measured, never eyeballed:

- **Fillet weld gauges** — leg length and throat size.
- **Undercut gauges such as the V-WAC** — undercut depth, porosity diameter.
- **Bridge cam (cambridge) gauge** — reinforcement height, undercut depth, bevel and fillet dimensions.
- **Hi-Lo gauge** — root opening and joint misalignment at fit-up.
- **Pit gauge** — depth of corrosion pits.
- **Steel scale and tape** — lengths, spacings, and locations from a datum.

Measure, write the number, and compare it to the limit; criteria are written as not-to-exceed values, so a measurement at the limit meets it — but record the actual value so nobody has to take your adjective for it. Know your authority: a Level I examines and records to written instructions, while final evaluation and disposition typically rest with a Level II or III, exactly as the employer's written practice assigns. When you mark hardware, use an approved marker — never a hard scribe, and respect material rules such as low-chloride markers on stainless steel.

sections.title: Defect Is a Verdict, Not a Synonym
sections.body: On a report, **defect** means exactly one thing: evaluated against the governing acceptance criteria and **rejectable**. Until that comparison happens, write **indication** or **discontinuity**. Loose use of the word can contractually condemn acceptable hardware — and a report that cried defect once gets argued with forever.

sections.heading: The Report: Written for a Stranger, Ten Years Out
sections.body:
A report works only if a stranger — different company, different decade — can reconstruct the examination from it alone. That is the test behind every required field:

- **What was examined** — part number, serial or heat number, weld or joint identification, drawing reference, and the extent: which surfaces, which lengths.
- **How** — procedure number and revision, technique (direct or remote), and any aids: mirror, magnifier, borescope.
- **Under what conditions** — illumination measured at the surface, the light meter's identification and calibration due date, and the surface condition or preparation.
- **Against what** — the acceptance criteria document, down to the paragraph or table.
- **What was found** — each recordable indication with type, size, and location dimensioned from a named datum: 415 mm from the north end of weld W12, never from a vanishing reference like a chalk mark.
- **The verdict** — accepted or rejected, item by item, and the disposition path for anything rejectable.
- **Who and when** — examiner name, signature, certification level, and date.

Most shops put this on a standard form precisely so nothing gets forgotten on a Friday afternoon. Fill every block: a blank field reads as either not done or not recorded, and both are audit findings. If a field truly does not apply, write that — N/A is information; blank is doubt.

sections.caption: A complete VT report ties one physical part to one procedure revision, proven examination conditions, relocatable findings, and an accountable examiner.
Minimum report contents — and the question each one answers.
rows: Part ID — serial, heat, weld number, drawing reference
What ruler were the results judged against?
Illumination reading, meter ID, calibration due date
Indication type, size, and location from a named datum
Can a stranger relocate the finding?
Disposition — accept or reject, item by item
Examiner name, signature, level, and date
Who is accountable, and were they qualified?

sections.heading: Records That Survive: Photos, Sketches, Corrections, Retention
sections.body:
Words locate things poorly, so good reports carry pictures. A **photograph** needs a scale reference in frame — a ruler or a gauge — sharp focus on the indication, a note of the camera position, and a wider context shot so the close-up can be found again. A **sketch** needs the datum drawn and named, the indication dimensioned from it, and orientation marked: a north arrow, flow direction, or top-of-pipe.

Paper discipline is part of the method. Write legibly, in permanent ink. Correct an error with a **single line through it, the correction beside it, your initials, and the date** — never a scribble, never white-out, because an obliterated number looks exactly like a hidden one. Sign only for work you personally performed or witnessed.

Then the record goes into retention for as long as the contract and the quality system require — often the life of the component. The ceremony exists because the record outlives the moment. It is the **baseline** the next in-service examination compares against to answer the only question that matters then: has this changed? It is the **evidence** in warranty disputes and failure investigations. And as far as the code is concerned, an examination that was never documented never happened.

figures.title: A VT Report That Stands on Its Own

interactive.title: Disposition Day: Call It Like the Criteria Say
interactive.intro: You are performing final visual testing on a structural fillet weld. The procedure invokes acceptance criteria: no cracks of any size; undercut not to exceed 1/32 in. (0.8 mm); no single pore over 3/32 in. (2.4 mm). Lighting is verified at 1,080 lux with an in-calibration meter. Work through the calls.

steps.prompt: Near the weld toe you spot a short, dark, linear mark. What is your first move?
options: Log it as a crack — linear means crack
Clean the area, re-examine under the required light, and interpret what the indication actually is
Ignore it — it is probably just slag staining
Grind it out so it cannot become a problem
steps.feedback: An observation is only an indication until you interpret it. Clean the surface and look again — slag smears, scratches, and cracks can all read as linear at first glance. Never grind and never ignore before interpretation.

steps.prompt: After cleaning, the linear mark is gone — it was smeared slag dust. How do you classify the original indication?
options: A defect, because it interfered with the examination
A relevant indication that corrected itself
A false indication caused by surface condition — re-examine the cleaned area and continue
A nonrelevant indication that must be repaired
steps.feedback: It did not come from a discontinuity in the part, so it is a false indication. Correct the cause, re-examine, and move on. Nothing gets repaired, because nothing is wrong.

steps.prompt: You find undercut along 2 in. of the toe. Your gauge reads 0.6 mm deep; the limit is 0.8 mm. What is the correct call?
options: Reject — undercut is never acceptable
Acceptable relevant indication — record the measured value and location as the procedure requires, and accept
Defect — any measurable undercut is a defect
No action — acceptable conditions are not recorded
steps.feedback: It is a real, relevant indication, but it measures inside the limit, so it is acceptable. Record what you measured and where — acceptable indications still go in the record when the procedure requires it, because they are tomorrow's baseline.

steps.prompt: A single pore in the cap measures about 1/8 in. (3.2 mm); the limit is 3/32 in. (2.4 mm). What happens now?
options: It becomes a defect: mark its location, document type, size, and position, and report it for disposition
Average it with the smaller pores nearby to see if it passes
Accept it, since porosity is only cosmetic
Quietly ask the welder to fix it and skip the paperwork
steps.feedback: A relevant indication that exceeds the criteria is a defect by definition. Mark it, document it completely, and report it. Repairs happen through the documented disposition process — never off the record.

checkQuestions.question: A confirmed crack 1.5 mm long is found in a weld governed by criteria that state 'no cracks of any size.' The correct classification is:
options: Relevant indication, acceptable because it is small
Nonrelevant indication
Defect — a rejectable discontinuity
False indication
checkQuestions.explanation: It is a real discontinuity, so the indication is relevant, and the criteria allow no cracks at all — evaluation makes it rejectable, which is the definition of a defect.

checkQuestions.question: A dark linear mark near a weld toe disappears completely after you solvent-clean the area. How should it be handled?
options: Record it as a repaired crack
Treat it as a false indication: correct the cause, re-examine the cleaned area, and continue
Reject the weld in case it comes back
Record it as a nonrelevant indication
checkQuestions.explanation: The indication came from surface contamination, not from the part — a false indication. You fix the cause, confirm the cleaned surface is sound, and move on. Nonrelevant indications come from real features; this came from nothing at all.

checkQuestions.question: Undercut measures 0.5 mm deep where the criteria allow up to 0.8 mm. What goes on the report?
options: Nothing — acceptable conditions are not reported
The measured value and location, recorded as an acceptable relevant indication per the procedure
A rejection, because undercut is always a defect
A note reading 'minor undercut, looks OK'
checkQuestions.explanation: It is a relevant indication that meets the criteria: accepted, but recorded with the actual measurement and location as the procedure requires. Adjectives without numbers help nobody ten years from now.

checkQuestions.question: Why does the report record the light meter reading and the meter's calibration due date?
options: To bill the customer for equipment usage
To prove the required examination conditions existed, measured with a traceable instrument
Because the meter is the most expensive item in the kit
To schedule the next meter purchase
checkQuestions.explanation: Results only mean something if the required conditions existed when they were taken. The reading proves the illumination, and the calibration status proves the reading itself can be trusted.

checkQuestions.question: You wrote the wrong weld number on a paper report. The correct fix is:
options: White-out over the error and write the new number
Discard the page and rewrite the report from memory later
A single line through the error, the correction beside it, your initials, and the date
Scribble the error out completely so it cannot be misread
checkQuestions.explanation: Quality records stay transparent: a single-line strikeout leaves the original visible while showing who corrected it, what to, and when. Obliterating an entry looks like hiding one.

references: Acceptance criteria per governing codes such as AWS D1.1 or ASME B31.3 (generic reference)
SNT-TC-1A — Level I records results; evaluation authority per the employer's written practice
